data.title: KKR explores potential $10B deal for ex-Unilever spread business - report
data.description:
KKR & Co. (KKR) is exploring a potential sale of its spreads business, formerly owned by Unilever (UL), in a deal that could value the unit at around $10B, according to a report by the Financial Times.

The business, now known as Flora Food Group, was carved out of Unilever’s (UL) portfolio and includes well-known margarine and spreads brands such as Flora and Country Crock.

The U.S. private equity firm is working with advisers on a potential sale of Flora and could draw interest from other buyout firms, the report added.

KKR (KKR) acquired the spreads unit from Unilever in 2017 for about €6.8B at that time, as the consumer goods giant sought to streamline its portfolio and focus on faster-growing categories.

The private equity firm previously attempted a sale at that valuation, but the process was shelved in April 2024 after negotiations with the Abu Dhabi sovereign wealth fund, ADQ, collapsed due to disagreements over pricing.

data.title: Pentagon’s $25B Iran war estimate seen as low, excludes base rebuilding costs: report
data.description:
The $25B estimate that a top Pentagon official gave to lawmakers on Wednesday for the total cost to date of the Iran war is a lowball figure that does not include the cost of repairing extensive damage suffered by US bases in the region, three people familiar with the matter told CNN.

One of the sources said the real cost estimate is closer to $40B-50B when accounting for the costs of rebuilding US military installations and replacing destroyed assets.

Iranian strikes across the Gulf in the early days of the war significantly damaged at least nine US military sites in just 48 hours, hitting facilities in Bahrain, Kuwait, Iraq, the UAE and Qatar, CNN has reported.

Several critical US radar systems and other equipment across the Middle East were also apparently destroyed by Iranian strikes. A US Air Force E-3 Sentry aircraft was also destroyed in an Iranian strike on a Saudi Arabia air base.

Jules “Jay” Hurst III, the Pentagon official currently working as the agency’s comptroller, told the House Armed Services Committee on Wednesday that “most” of the $25B cost he cited has been spent on munitions, and Secretary of Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth declined to say whether that figure included repairing damage to US bases.

Democratic Rep. Ro Khanna was skeptical of the $25B estimate, calling it “totally off” during Wednesday’s hearing, as per the report. Pentagon officials had previously told Congress the war cost roughly $11B in just the first six days alone, and the department asked the White House last month to approve a request to Congress for over $200B in additional military funding for the ongoing war, as per earlier reports.

During budget briefings for reporters last week, Hurst said the Pentagon does not “have a final number for what the damage is to our installations overseas,” and it depends “on how we decide to rebuild those, or if we do.”

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Conmed projects 2026 organic growth of 5.0% to 6.5% while refinancing debt and exiting GI
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: CONMED (CNMD) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "During the first quarter, we reached an agreement to divest certain GI products. And in April, we reached a second agreement to divest our remaining GI products." (President, CEO & Director Patrick Beyer) "This decision was intentional and strategic. It allows us to concentrate resources and investment on our higher growth, higher-margin offerings and better focuses the organization on driving improved execution and delivering long-term shareholder value." (President, CEO & Director Beyer)
  • "Total sales for the quarter were $317 million, a decrease of 1.3% compared to the prior year quarter." (President, CEO & Director Beyer) "Excluding the impact of our previously announced exit from our gastroenterology product lines, total sales increased 3.8% year-over-year as reported and 2.1% in constant currency." (President, CEO & Director Beyer)
  • "From an earnings perspective, excluding special items that affected comparability, our adjusted net income of $27.1 million decreased 8.5% year-over-year, and our adjusted diluted net earnings per share of $0.89 decreased 6.3% year-over-year." (President, CEO & Director Beyer)
  • "AirSeal benefits from a large installed base of over 10,000 systems globally" (President, CEO & Director Beyer) and "today, AirSeal is used only in 6% to 7% of those cases" in U.S. laparoscopy. (President, CEO & Director Beyer) "Taken together...support our confidence that AirSeal can deliver high single-digit to low double-digit growth over the long term." (President, CEO & Director Beyer)
  • "On the legislative front, we now have 20 U.S. states with smoke-free operating room laws on the books, covering approximately 51% of the population." (President, CEO & Director Beyer) "Our strategic focus is on direct smoke evacuation, where we control the customer relationship and capture the full margin profile." (President, CEO & Director Beyer)
  • "The search for our new CFO is progressing well, and we look forward to providing you with an update soon." (President, CEO & Director Beyer)
  • "Adjusted gross margin for the first quarter was 57.4%, which is 100 basis points higher than the prior year quarter, driven by favorable product mix and positive foreign currency impact." (Advisor Todd Garner)

Outlook

  • "We are pleased to be able to raise our organic growth expectation for 2026 to 5.0% to 6.5% from our prior range of 4.5% to 6.0%." (Advisor Garner) "We now expect foreign exchange rates to be a tailwind to revenue of between 40 and 50 basis points." (Advisor Garner)
  • "With these 2 agreements complete and happening faster than originally anticipated, our 2026 revenue guidance for the GI product lines is now between $14.5 million and $17.5 million." (Advisor Garner) "That results in expected reported revenue between $1.35 billion to $1.375 billion for 2026." (Advisor Garner) "We expect reported revenue in Q2 to be between $336 million and $340 million." (Advisor Garner)
  • "Our intent is to refinance with bank debt, which we expect could increase our full year adjusted interest expense, impacting adjusted EPS for the full year by at least $0.10." (Advisor Garner) "Despite this increase...we are able to keep our adjusted EPS guidance for the full year unchanged at the range of $4.30 to $4.45." (Advisor Garner) "For Q2, we expect adjusted EPS to be between $1.09 and $1.14." (Advisor Garner)

Financial Results

  • "For the first quarter of 2026, our total sales decreased 2.9% year-over-year. Organic sales increased 2.1% year-over-year." (Advisor Garner) "For Q1, total sales in the U.S. decreased 5.8% versus the prior year quarter, and total international sales grew 1.0%." (Advisor Garner)
  • "Total worldwide orthopedic sales grew 4.5% in the first quarter" while "total worldwide general surgery sales decreased 8.5% in the quarter" and "organic worldwide general surgery sales were flat over prior year." (Advisor Garner)
  • "The OEM smoke products were again a meaningful headwind in Q1." (Advisor Garner) "These non-focused products for us can be very lumpy quarter-to-quarter, and that was the biggest drag on general surgery sales in Q1." (Advisor Garner)
  • "Cash flow provided from operations in the quarter was $13.5 million compared to $41.5 million in the first quarter of 2025." (Advisor Garner) "In Q1, we bought back approximately 858,000 shares for a total of $37.4 million." (Advisor Garner)
  • "Our leverage ratio on March 31 was 3.1x." (Advisor Garner) "We continue to expect operating cash flow for the full year to be between $145 million and $155 million and capital expenditures between $20 million and $30 million, resulting in free cash flow around $125 million." (Advisor Garner)

Q&A

  • Gracia Mahoney, BofA Securities: Asked about refinancing strategy and mitigating EPS impact into 2027; Advisor Garner said, "we determined that it was not in the best interest of CONMED shareholders to do new convertible notes at this time" and added, "as I said in my script, we see that as at least $0.10" of adjusted EPS impact, while "the strength in the business the results of Q1 allow us to keep EPS the same." (Advisor Garner)
  • Gracia Mahoney, BofA Securities: Asked about inflation pressure; President, CEO & Director Beyer said, "We are seeing some pressure on some commodity products like oil, gold that are affecting our cost of goods sold" and "we don't expect any more of the macro influences on the cost side to impact our guidance here." (President, CEO & Director Beyer)
  • Ross Osborn, Wells Fargo: Asked AirSeal DV5 attach rate; President, CEO & Director Beyer said, "We have guided between 10% and 20%, and we continue to be in that zone." (President, CEO & Director Beyer)
  • Ross Osborn, Wells Fargo: Asked about visibility into smoke-evac legislation tailwinds; President, CEO & Director Beyer said, "We have line of sight of 13 additional states have bills pending" and "we believe Maryland and Massachusetts are the most likely ones to pass." (President, CEO & Director Beyer)
  • Robert Marcus, JPMorgan: Pressed on Q2 revenue step-up bridge; management pointed to acceleration in orthopedics and growth drivers, with President, CEO & Director Beyer saying, "our orthopedic business and BioBrace will continue to accelerate its growth" and "you can expect the orthopedic business to continue to accelerate." (President, CEO & Director Beyer)
  • Robert Marcus, JPMorgan: Asked gross margin durability after Q1 strength; Advisor Garner said, "Our full year guide for gross margin was 50 to 100 for the year" and "we think we should be in that 50 to 100 every quarter." (Advisor Garner)
  • Anna Runci, Piper Sandler: Asked what gates U.S. laparoscopic penetration and investments; President, CEO & Director Beyer said, "we have an active program in the United States around standardization and laparoscopy" and "we put over 50% more in quarter 1 in the market than we did in quarter 1 2025." (President, CEO & Director Beyer)
  • Michael Matson, Needham: Asked how long OEM smoke is a drag and its size; President, CEO & Director Beyer said, "the Buffalo Filter piece of our smoke evacuation is smaller than our direct" and "we believe over time, it will continue to get smaller." (President, CEO & Director Beyer)
  • Michael Matson, Needham: Asked whether refinancing implies a 2027 headwind; Advisor Garner said, "We don't want to get ahead of ourselves" and "we'll give '27 guidance at the right time." (Advisor Garner)

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were slightly negative to neutral in tone, with repeated probing on refinancing/EPS sensitivity and the Q2 growth bridge, including a follow-up that the step-up "seems like a larger-than-normal step-up in dollars." (Robert Marcus, JPMorgan)
  • Management tone was slightly positive and execution-focused, while defending guidance assumptions; on refinancing, Advisor Garner emphasized uncertainty with "I'm not being terribly precise" and "we don't know exactly what we're going to get." (Advisor Garner)
  • Compared with Q4 2025, the current call carried more transitional language around finance leadership and capital structure (CFO search and refinancing), while Q4’s tone leaned more on completed 2025 performance and initial 2026 setup.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The GI exit moved from an announced strategic decision in Q4 to completed sales and tighter 2026 GI revenue expectations in Q1, with management saying the divestitures occurred "faster than originally anticipated" and updated the remaining 2026 GI revenue outlook. (Advisor Garner)
  • 2026 organic growth guidance was raised in Q1 to "5.0% to 6.5%" from Q4’s "4.5% to 6%." (Advisor Garner)
  • The CFO transition advanced from Q4’s planned handoff (CFO remaining through transition) to Q1’s statement that "The search for our new CFO is progressing well," with Todd Garner participating as "adviser." (President, CEO & Director Beyer)
  • Analyst focus shifted from Q4 questions on cadence and portfolio review to Q1 questions centered on refinancing mechanics, inflation/cost pressure, and the Q1-to-Q2 revenue bridge.

Risks and Concerns

  • "We are seeing some pressure on some commodity products like oil, gold that are affecting our cost of goods sold" and "some component prices go up," with mitigation described as working "with our vendors and our partners and our supply chain." (President, CEO & Director Beyer)
  • "The OEM smoke products were again a meaningful headwind in Q1" and were described as "very lumpy quarter-to-quarter." (Advisor Garner)
  • "Our intent is to refinance with bank debt" rather than issuing convertibles, with the company expecting the move could impact adjusted EPS "by at least $0.10." (Advisor Garner)

Final Takeaway

CONMED’s management emphasized that the GI divestitures are now substantially complete and intended to sharpen focus on AirSeal, Buffalo Filter/direct smoke, and BioBrace, while 2026 organic growth guidance was raised to 5.0% to 6.5% and full-year adjusted EPS guidance was maintained at $4.30 to $4.45 despite an expected refinancing-driven interest headwind of at least $0.10. Management also pointed to supply chain progress supporting orthopedics momentum, ongoing legislative tailwinds for smoke evacuation, and a CFO search that is "progressing well."

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Northeast Community Bancorp GAAP EPS of $0.74 misses by $0.01, revenue of $24.92M misses by $1.5M
data.description:
• Northeast Community Bancorp press release (NECB): Q1 GAAP EPS of $0.74 misses by $0.01.
  • Revenue of $24.92M (-1.4% Y/Y) misses by $1.5M.
  • Allowance for credit losses related to loans totaled $4.6 million, or 0.25% total loans at March 31, 2026 compared to $4.7 million, or 0.25% of total loans at December 31, 2025.
  • Loans, net of the allowance for credit losses, decreased $31.8 million, or 1.7%, to $1.8 billion at March 31, 2026 from $1.9 billion at December 31, 2025.
  • Total deposits increased $9.4 million, or 0.6%, to $1.6 billion at March 31, 2026 from $1.6 billion at December 31, 2025.

data.title: Climb Global Solutions Non-GAAP EPS of $0.19 misses by $0.06, revenue of $182.4M beats by $31.06M
data.description:
• Climb Global Solutions press release (CLMB): Q1 Non-GAAP EPS of $0.19 misses by $0.06.
  • Revenue of $182.4M (+32.1% Y/Y) beats by $31.06M.
  • Adjusted EBITDA (a non-GAAP financial measure defined below) increased 4% to $7.9 million.
  • Gross billings (a key operational metric defined below) increased 14% to $542.8 million. Distribution segment gross billings increased 15% to $520.9 million, and Solutions segment gross billings increased 4% to $21.9 million.

data.title: Mesoblast reports Q1 results
data.description:
• Mesoblast press release (MESO): Q1 Ryoncil® gross sales for the quarter were US$35.3 million, and net revenues were US$30.3 million.
  • Strong sales in February and March offset holiday seasonality in January.
  • Revenue generated during this first year of Ryoncil® launch approaches US$100 million.
  • Net operating cash spend for the quarter was US$4.1 million. The reduction in net operating cash spend was driven by receipts of US$34.6 million and tight control of operating expenses.
  • Mesoblast had US$122 million of cash at March 31, 2026.

data.title: California Water Service GAAP EPS of $0.07 misses by $0.16, revenue of $214.57M beats by $4.3M
data.description:
• California Water Service press release (CWT): Q1 GAAP EPS of $0.07 misses by $0.16.
  • Revenue of $214.57M (+5.2% Y/Y) beats by $4.3M.

data.title: Anthropic eyes fresh funding at $900B valuation, set to surpass OpenAI - report
data.description:
Anthropic (ANTHRO) is considering a new funding round that could value the AI developer at more than $900B, according to a Bloomberg report that cites people familiar with the matter.

The company behind the Claude chatbot is looking at proposals that could more than double its current valuation, noting that no offers have been accepted and discussions remain preliminary, the report added.

The fundraising push ⁠comes ahead of a potential IPO, which may be launched as soon as October. Anthropic (ANTHRO) had reportedly declined multiple unsolicited investor proposals earlier for a funding round that would have valued the company at $800B or higher.

The AI firm has been on a hunt for more infrastructure to meet growing demand for its products. However, backing from major tech companies forms a key pillar of its capital base. 

Anthropic’s (ANTHRO) rapid rise has heightened competition in the AI sector, especially with OpenAI (OPENAI), which was last valued at $852 billion in a March funding round.

data.title: Compañía de Minas Buenaventura GAAP EPS of $1.32, revenue of $624.6M misses by $26.9M
data.description:
• Compañía de Minas Buenaventura press release (BVN): Q1 GAAP EPS of $1.32.
  • Revenue of $624.6M (+103.0% Y/Y) misses by $26.9M.
  • EBITDA from direct operations was US$ 386.3 million, compared to US$ 126.3 million reported in 1Q25.
  • Capital expenditures related to San Gabriel totaled US$49.2 million in 1Q26.
  • Buenaventura's cash position totaled US$ 759.9 million as of March 31, 2026.
  • The company reported a net cash position, with net debt of negative US$51.9 million, equivalent to a leverage ratio of -0.05x.

data.title: SoftBank’s $40B loan for OpenAI stake draws more banks: report
data.description:
• Banks that recently signed a $40B bridge loan with SoftBank Group (SFTBY) for its investment in U.S. tech giant OpenAI (OPENAI) have attracted more lenders to the deal in syndication, Bloomberg News reported.
  • At least eight banks had submitted commitments as sub-underwriters as of late last week, the report said.
  • It’s common for the initial lenders on a deal to distribute loans to a broader group. HSBC Holdings (HSBC), BNP Paribas SA, and Intesa Sanpaolo SpA (IITSF) (ISNPY) are among the new joiners, the report added.
